content.text: What It Is

An Agent Templates Catalog is a curated, versioned library of pre-built agent blueprints that development teams can discover, clone, and customize. It functions like an internal registry of proven patterns — from simple tool-calling agents to complex multi-agent orchestration topologies — enabling teams to start from validated baselines rather than blank files.

Each template in the catalog captures not just the agent code but also its configuration schema, tool definitions, test fixtures, deployment manifests, and compatibility metadata. A developer browsing the catalog can search by capability tag ("rag", "streaming", "code-review"), filter by supported model family, and scaffold a new project in seconds using a CLI command or a self-service portal.

The catalog sits inside the Internal Developer Platform (IDP) alongside other golden-path assets such as service templates and infrastructure modules. It is maintained by a platform team but contributed to by any team that has built an agent pattern worth generalizing.
Why It Matters

Agentic applications involve an unusually large number of moving parts: model selection, system prompt design, tool schema definitions, memory management, retry logic, streaming configuration, evaluation harnesses, and deployment manifests. When every team invents this structure from scratch, the organization accumulates a long tail of subtly incompatible patterns that are hard to audit, upgrade, or secure centrally.

A templates catalog solves this by making the right way the easy way. A team that needs a code-review agent starts from the "code-review-agent" template that already has the correct tool schema, a tested system prompt, and integration with the IDP's observability stack. They spend their time on domain-specific customization, not on re-discovering how to wire up streaming or how to handle tool call retries.
Benefits
Speed to first working agent.
Teams scaffold production-grade structure in minutes, not days.
Built-in quality.
Templates carry the platform team's security policies, logging hooks, and retry patterns by default.
Centralized upgrades.
When a vulnerability is found in the base agent runtime, one template update can be propagated across all derived projects.
Institutional knowledge retention.
Proven patterns from successful projects become reusable assets rather than tribal knowledge locked in a single team.
Onboarding acceleration.
New engineers learn idiomatic agent patterns by reading and running catalog templates.
Cross-team discoverability.
Teams can discover what others have built without reading Confluence pages or asking in Slack.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Template maintenance burden.
Templates go stale as model APIs and IDP conventions evolve. Someone must own each template's lifecycle.
Premature abstraction risk.
Generalizing too early produces overly complex templates that are harder to use than starting from scratch.
False confidence.
Teams may assume a template is production-ready when it only covers the happy path. Test coverage in templates must be honest about what is and is not covered.
Divergence after forking.
Once a team customizes a template, keeping in sync with upstream changes is a manual effort unless the catalog has an update propagation mechanism.
Discovery friction.
A poorly tagged or documented catalog is as bad as no catalog — developers default to copy-paste from colleagues instead.
How It Works

A template in the catalog is a directory structure that follows a well-defined manifest contract. The root manifest file declares the template's identity, version, parameters, and compatibility constraints. A code-generation step reads the manifest and user-supplied parameter values, renders the template (Cookiecutter-style or Copier-style), and writes the scaffolded project to disk.

The catalog itself is typically stored as a Git repository (or a monorepo subdirectory), with each template in its own folder. A registry service indexes the templates, stores metadata, and exposes a search API used by both the CLI and the developer portal. Usage metrics (how many times each template was scaffolded) feed back into the portal's ranking and the platform team's maintenance prioritization.
Template Structure
A well-structured agent template contains the following files at minimum:
manifest.yaml
— template identity, version, parameters, model compatibility
agent/
— agent entrypoint, system prompt, tool definitions
tools/
— tool implementation stubs or full implementations
tests/
— unit tests for tool logic and integration tests with recorded traces
deploy/
— Kubernetes manifests or IDP deployment config
README.md
— usage guide, parameter reference, and known limitations

# manifest.yaml — agent template manifest
name: rag-agent
version: 2.1.0
description: |
  Retrieval-augmented generation agent with streaming support.
  Connects to a vector store and synthesizes cited answers.
author: platform-team
tags:
  - rag
  - streaming
  - tool-calling
  - citations
compatibility:
  models:
    - claude-3-5-sonnet-*
    - claude-3-haiku-*
  min_sdk_version: "0.30.0"
parameters:
  - name: agent_name
    type: string
    description: Name of the generated agent module
    required: true
  - name: vector_store
    type: enum
    values: [pinecone, weaviate, pgvector]
    default: pgvector
    description: Vector store backend to use
  - name: top_k
    type: integer
    default: 5
    description: Number of chunks to retrieve per query
  - name: enable_streaming
    type: boolean
    default: true
    description: Enable streaming token output
files:
  - src: agent/agent.py.jinja
    dest: "{{ agent_name }}/agent.py"
  - src: tools/retrieve.py
    dest: "{{ agent_name }}/tools/retrieve.py"
  - src: tests/
    dest: "{{ agent_name }}/tests/"
  - src: deploy/deployment.yaml.jinja
    dest: deploy/{{ agent_name }}-deployment.yaml
postscaffold:
  - pip install -r requirements.txt
  - python -m pytest {{ agent_name }}/tests/ -q
Architecture and Design

attrs.alt: Agent Templates Catalog architecture diagram showing discovery to deployment flow

content.text: The architecture has three planes. The control plane is the Template Registry: a versioned Git repository indexed by a registry service that exposes a REST API. The delivery plane is the Code Generator: a CLI and web portal that read manifest parameters, render Jinja2 templates, and write the project scaffold. The runtime plane is the IDP's deployment pipeline, which the generated scaffold integrates with automatically.

Templates are semantically versioned. A breaking change in the manifest schema or the generated output increments the major version. Additive parameter additions increment the minor version. Bug fixes in template content increment the patch version. The compatibility matrix in each manifest declares which model families and SDK versions the template supports, allowing the registry to warn users who try to scaffold with an incompatible combination.
When to Use It

An Agent Templates Catalog delivers the most value when multiple teams in the same organization are building agentic applications and would otherwise each solve the same structural problems independently. The inflection point is typically around three to five teams: below that, informal knowledge sharing through shared repositories and code review is sufficient; above that, the lack of a catalog results in measurable duplication, inconsistent security posture, and slow onboarding.
Use a catalog when more than one team is building agents with similar structural needs (tool calling, memory, streaming).
Use a catalog when the platform team needs to enforce security or compliance requirements across all agent deployments.
Use a catalog when new team members are spending more than a day understanding how to structure a new agent project.
Defer a catalog when only one team is building agents, or when the agent patterns are too immature to generalize without over-constraining future designs.
How to Implement

Start with two or three templates that capture patterns already proven in production. Do not design templates in the abstract — extract them from real projects. The first template should be the simplest possible agentic pattern (a single-agent tool caller), not the most sophisticated one.
Identify two to three proven agent patterns from existing projects. Inspect what varies between instances and what is always the same.
Write a manifest.yaml for each pattern, parameterizing only what genuinely varies. Keep the parameter surface small at first.
Convert the pattern to Jinja2 (or Copier) templates and write a post-scaffold script that installs dependencies and runs the test suite.
Add CI that validates the manifest schema, renders the template with default parameters, and verifies the generated project passes its own tests.
Build or adopt a CLI command (e.g., using Copier or a custom tool) that developers use to scaffold from the catalog.
Publish the catalog to the IDP's developer portal with a search and filter UI. Track usage metrics per template.
Assign template owners. A template without an owner becomes a liability — it drifts out of date and erodes trust in the catalog.
Examples
Scaffolding from the Catalog via CLI

# Install the platform CLI
pip install idp-cli

# List available agent templates
idp agent templates list
# Output:
# NAME                    VERSION   TAGS                      USAGES
# base-tool-calling       1.4.0     tool-calling, streaming   142
# rag-agent               2.1.0     rag, streaming, citations  89
# code-review-agent       1.0.3     code, github, review       34
# supervisor-workers      0.9.0     orchestration, multi-agent 12
# data-analysis-agent     1.1.0     data, pandas, sql          28

# Search by tag
idp agent templates list --tag rag

# Scaffold a new project from the rag-agent template
idp agent scaffold rag-agent \
  --name my-support-agent \
  --vector-store pgvector \
  --top-k 8 \
  --enable-streaming true \
  --output-dir ./agents/my-support-agent

# The generated project structure:
# my-support-agent/
#   agent.py              # main agent with system prompt and tool loop
#   tools/
#     retrieve.py         # vector store retrieval tool
#     synthesize.py       # answer synthesis with citations
#   tests/
#     test_retrieve.py    # unit tests for retrieval tool
#     test_agent.py       # integration tests with recorded traces
#   requirements.txt
#   pyproject.toml
# deploy/
#   my-support-agent-deployment.yaml  # K8s manifest pre-wired for IDP

# Run generated tests immediately
cd agents/my-support-agent
pip install -r requirements.txt
pytest tests/ -v
Customizing a Template After Scaffolding
Real-World Example

A platform engineering team at a mid-sized SaaS company maintains a catalog of twelve agent templates. Before the catalog existed, each product team spent roughly two weeks building agent scaffolding from scratch, and the resulting structures differed enough that the security team had to audit each one separately for credential handling and rate limiting.

After introducing the catalog, new agent projects scaffold in under five minutes. The catalog's base templates pre-wire credential injection from the IDP's secret store, instrument the agent with OpenTelemetry traces, and include a built-in circuit breaker for external tool calls. The security team now audits the three base templates rather than fifteen individual agent codebases — a reduction that freed two weeks of security review time per quarter.

The most-used template ("rag-agent") has been scaffolded 89 times across 14 teams. When the vector store client library released a breaking API change, the platform team updated the template and published a migration guide. Teams on the old version had a clear upgrade path rather than each team having to diagnose the same breaking change independently.
Common Mistakes
Over-parameterizing early.
Adding 20 parameters to cover every possible variation makes templates harder to use than starting from scratch. Prefer opinionated defaults with clear extension points.
No template owner.
Templates without assigned owners go stale within months. Stale templates are worse than no catalog because they create a false sense of security.
Skipping tests in the template.
A template that generates an untested project signals to users that testing is optional. Templates should include working tests that cover the scaffolded agent's core behavior.
Treating the catalog as a one-way door.
Teams should be able to contribute templates back from their own projects. A read-only catalog controlled by a single team becomes a bottleneck and loses community buy-in.
Poor discovery metadata.
Templates with no tags, vague descriptions, or no usage examples will not be found. Invest in metadata as seriously as in the template code itself.
Best Practices
Extract templates from two or three real production agents, not from theoretical requirements. Real usage surfaces what parameters actually vary.
Validate templates in CI by rendering them with default parameters and running the generated project's test suite on every pull request.
Version templates using semantic versioning and maintain a changelog. Teams need to know what changed and whether they need to migrate.
Include a compatibility matrix in every manifest. Teams should not have to guess whether a template works with their target model or SDK version.
Publish usage metrics to the developer portal. High-usage templates get more maintenance attention; low-usage templates are candidates for retirement.
Create a contribution process for teams to promote their own patterns into the catalog. Peer review and CI gates maintain quality without creating a bottleneck.
Performance and Scalability

The catalog itself is not on the critical path of agent runtime performance — it is used at development time, not at inference time. Scaffold operations complete in seconds. The registry service is read-heavy (developers browse and search far more than they contribute), so caching the manifest index in memory and serving it from a CDN is sufficient for most organizations.

At scale (hundreds of templates, thousands of developers), the main scalability challenge is catalog quality rather than registry throughput. As the number of templates grows, discoverability degrades if metadata discipline is not maintained. A template scoring system that combines usage count, recency of updates, and test coverage rate helps surface high-quality templates and deprioritize stale ones.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Copier
Template rendering engine
Supports update propagation to derived projects via git diff
Backstage Software Catalog
Developer portal and catalog UI
Template scaffolding via Software Templates plugin
GitHub / GitLab
Template source of truth and CI
PR-based contribution workflow; CI validates manifests
Custom IDP CLI
Primary scaffolding interface
Wraps Copier, injects platform-specific defaults
Jinja2 / Copier templates
Template authoring format
Conditionals and loops enable rich parameterization
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strength
Weakness
Templates Catalog (this)
Versioned, discoverable, CI-validated, platform-integrated
Maintenance overhead; risk of templates drifting stale
Copy-paste from colleagues
Zero setup cost
Untracked, stale, inconsistent; bugs propagate silently
Cookiecutter templates
Simple and widely understood
No update propagation to derived projects; no registry
Shared base library (SDK)
Changes propagate via package update
Less flexible for structural variation; requires careful API design
No catalog
Maximum flexibility; zero maintenance
Each team reinvents the wheel; security posture inconsistent
Security and Reliability

Templates are a force-multiplier for security: a security control baked into a well-used template propagates to every project that scaffolds from it. The corollary is that a security flaw in a template propagates just as widely — so template changes must go through the same review process as production code changes.
Never put credentials in templates. Templates should reference the IDP's secret store by environment variable name or secret reference, not by value.
Include rate limiting and circuit breaker boilerplate in base templates so all generated agents are resilient to downstream tool failures by default.
Sign template releases (e.g., with git tag signing) and validate signatures in the CLI before rendering. This prevents a compromised registry from serving malicious templates.
Include an SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) step in the post-scaffold script so each generated project has a dependency audit baseline from day one.
Mental Model

Think of a templates catalog the same way a construction firm thinks about standardized building plans. An architect does not design a structural foundation from first principles for every building — they start from a code-compliant foundation plan and customize the layout above it. The foundation plan is not the building; it is the correct starting point that lets the architect focus creative energy on the parts that actually differentiate the building.

An Agent Templates Catalog plays the same role. The templates are not the agents; they are the correct starting points that let developers focus their energy on the domain-specific logic — the system prompt, the tool implementations, the evaluation criteria — rather than on structural plumbing that every agent needs but none should have to reinvent.
Key Takeaways
An Agent Templates Catalog is a versioned library of pre-built agent blueprints that teams scaffold from, not copy-paste from.
Each template carries a manifest.yaml, parameterized source files, test fixtures, and deployment config — not just code.
Templates should be extracted from proven production agents, not designed from theoretical requirements.
Validate templates in CI by rendering them and running the generated project's tests on every change.
Assign template owners. A template without an owner is a liability, not an asset.
Security controls embedded in base templates propagate to all derived projects — making the catalog a force-multiplier for platform security policy.
The catalog's value is highest when multiple teams need similar patterns. Below that threshold, informal sharing is sufficient.
